perturbation.original_text: Each Participating Entity agrees that, in ad dition to whatever other remedies may be available to the other Participating Entities under applicable law, the other Participating Entities shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief with respect to any actual or threatened violation of this Agreement by a Participating Entity or any thir d party receiving Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Participating Entity agrees that, in addition to whatever other remedies may be available to the other Participating Entities under applicable law, the other Participating Entities may, at their sole discretion, seek injunctive relief with respect to any actual violation of this Agreement by a Participating Entity or any third party receiving Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief' to 'may, at their sole discretion, seek injunctive relief' and limiting it to actual violations, the contract diminishes the legal recourse available. The addition of 'at their sole discretion' implies that seeking injunctive relief is optional even in cases where it would be the appropriate remedy. Restricting it to actual violation further limits the option to take injunctive relief on 'threatened' violations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure - Rule 65
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65 governs the issuance of preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders. The modified text, by making injunctive relief discretionary and limiting it to actual violations, potentially contradicts the spirit and application of Rule 65, which allows for injunctive relief to prevent irreparable harm even in the face of threatened violations. The FRCP outlines specific criteria for obtaining injunctive relief, which may be undermined by the contract's discretionary language.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief,' indicating a clear right to pursue such relief. The modified text replaces this with 'may, at their sole discretion, seek injunctive relief,' introducing discretion where the original text implied an entitlement. Furthermore, the original text covers 'any actual or threatened violation,' while the modified text only addresses 'actual violation.' This significantly weakens the ability to seek injunctive relief for potential future harm, contradicting the spirit of FRCP 65, which allows for injunctions to prevent irreparable harm from even threatened violations, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'injunctions and restraining orders,' which implies a legal framework for their use, undermined by making the pursuit of injunctive relief discretionary.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information” und er this Agreement consists of: (i) all information disclosed by any Participati ng Entity, or any of its employees, directors, officers, affiliates, partners, agents, advisors or other representatives (“Representatives”) pursuant to that Participating Enti ty’s participation in or contribution to the development or implementation of a Cyber Mutual Assistance Prog ram, including any Participating Entity’s request for or provision of cyber mutual assistance, whether disclosed prior to or following the execution of this Agreement; (ii) any information or documentation produced by a Participating Entity, or any of its Representatives, under any Cyber Mutual Assistance Program or related to a specific request for or response to cyber mutual assistance, including any analysis of such information, and whether produced prior to or following the execution of this Agreement; (iii) any aggregation, consolidation , or listing of information or documentation disclosed by one or more Participating Entities, or any of their respective Representatives, pursuant to the development or implementation of a Cyber Mutual Assistance Program incl uding any Participating Entity’s request for or provision of cyber mutual assistance; and Final June 2016 2 (iv) all observations of equipm ent (including computer screens) and oral disclosures related to the development of any Cyber Mutual Assistance Program or a specific request for or response to cyber mutual assistance, including the systems, operations, and activities of each Participating Entity, whether such observations or oral di sclosures were made prior to or following the execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information” under this Agreement consists of: (i) all information disclosed by any Participating Entity, or any of its employees, directors, officers, affiliates, partners, agents, advisors or other representatives (“Representatives”) pursuant to that Participating Entity’s participation in or contribution to the development or implementation of a Cyber Mutual Assistance Program, including any Participating Entity’s request for or provision of cyber mutual assistance, whether disclosed prior to or following the execution of this Agreement; (ii) any information or documentation produced by a Participating Entity, or any of its Representatives, under any Cyber Mutual Assistance Program or related to a specific request for or response to cyber mutual assistance, including any analysis of such information, and whether produced prior to or following the execution of this Agreement; (iii) any aggregation, consolidation, or listing of information or documentation disclosed by one or more Participating Entities, or any of their respective Representatives, pursuant to the development or implementation of a Cyber Mutual Assistance Program including any Participating Entity’s request for or provision of cyber mutual assistance; and Final June 2016 2 (iv) all observations of equipment (including computer screens) and oral disclosures related to the development of any Cyber Mutual Assistance Program or a specific request for or response to cyber mutual assistance, excluding trade secrets, regardless of the systems, operations, and activities of each Participating Entity, whether such observations or oral disclosures were made prior to or following the execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract weakens the protection afforded to potentially valuable proprietary information. This exclusion creates ambiguity and could allow for the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets without violating the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) defines 'trade secret' broadly. By excluding 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract creates a contradiction. The DTSA aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation, but the contract's exclusion weakens this protection, potentially allowing for the legal misappropriation of trade secrets under the agreement's terms.

perturbation.justification: The contract modification explicitly excludes 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. This exclusion weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, which the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) aims to protect. The Law Explanation states that this exclusion potentially allows for the legal misappropriation of trade secrets under the agreement's terms, which contradicts the intent of the DTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, indicating that this Act is relevant. Scraped Law Snippet 2 provides a definition section of the US code related to trade secrets (18 USC 1839), implying the existence of legal protections for trade secrets. By carving out trade secrets from the definition of confidential information, the modified contract creates a loophole where misappropriation could be argued as permissible under the contract, directly contradicting the protections intended by trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, all Confidential Information in the possession or contro l of a Participating Entity and its Representatives that received such information shall be returned to the Participating Entity that disclosed the information, including all copies of such info rmation in any form whatsoever, unless otherwise instructed in writing by the Participating Entity tha t disclosed the information. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if the Confidential Information is retain ed in the computer backup system of a Participating Final June 2016 4 Entity, the Confidential Information will be destroye d in accordance with the regular ongoing records retention process of the Participating Entity. In lieu of return, a Partic ipating Entity may certify to the other Participating Entities in writ ing that all such Confidential Info rmation, in any form whatsoever, has been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, all Confidential Information in the possession or control of a Participating Entity and its Representatives that received such information shall be returned to the Participating Entity that disclosed the information, including all copies of such information in any form whatsoever, unless otherwise instructed in writing by the Participating Entity that disclosed the information. If the Confidential Information is retained in the computer backup system of a Participating Final June 2016 4 Entity, the Confidential Information will be maintained indefinitely. In lieu of return, a Participating Entity may certify to the other Participating Entities in writing that all such Confidential Information, in any form whatsoever, has been permanently stored and secured.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the requirement for destruction of Confidential Information in backup systems to indefinite maintenance, the contract creates a potential conflict with data privacy regulations. The original clause aimed to limit the retention period, while the modified version could lead to excessive data retention and increased risk of data breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 5(1)(e) mandates that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. The modified text, by allowing for indefinite retention of Confidential Information (which may contain personal data), contradicts this principle of storage limitation under the GDPR. This is irrespective of this document following US law, because if it is to be compatible for business with any EU entity, the GDPR still applies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: f of the European Commission is responsible for the use which might be made of the following information. As this guidance reflects the state of the art at the time of its drafting, it should be regarded as a 'living tool' open for improvement and its content may be subject to modifications without notice. International dimension of data protection The EU has established international data protection agreements to ensure that EU citizens' personal data remains protected even if transferred outside the EU. EU data protection legislation includes safeguards for when transferring data to third countries, including adequacy decisions, standard contractual clauses (SCC) and binding corporate rules (BCR). Find out more about the international dimension of data protection Funding to support the implementation of the GDPR The Commission has provided funding to national data protection authorities to finance projects that support the implementation of the GDPR. These projects aim to equip individuals and businesses with the knowledge and resources needed to navigate and ensure compliance with data protection rules. The types of projects funded by the Commission typically include awareness-raising campaigns, training programs, and the development of practical tools and materials that can facilitate small and medium-sized enterprises' (SMEs) compliance with the GDPR. Find out more about these EU-funded projects Simplifying compliance with the GDPR through reduced record-keeping obligations In May 2025, the Commission adopted a Single Market Simplification proposal to cut €300 million in annual administrative cost for companies. In order to respond to calls from Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) and Small and Mid-Caps companies (SMCs), the Commission introduced targeted changes to the rules on record-keeping for small and medium-sized companies and organisations under 750 employees. They include namely: Extending the derogation from record-keeping obligation under Article 30(5) of the GDPR to SMCs and organisations under 750 employees. Requiring SMEs, SMCs and organisations with fewer than 750 employees to maintain records only when their processing of personal data can be considered “high risk” under the GDPR. The proposal ensures to maintain robust standards of data protection and respects the GDPR risk-based approach. It does not affect the rest of the provisions of the GDPR. perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates indefinite maintenance of confidential information, which contradicts the principle of storage limitation under GDPR, as explained in the Law Explanation. While no specific law snippet is directly quoted, the Law Explanation's reference to GDPR Article 5(1)(e), which mandates data retention for no longer than necessary, combined with the perturbation explanation highlighting potential conflicts with data privacy regulations, supports the conclusion that the modified text creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating data protection principles.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding Sections 1 and 2, a Participating Entity shall not have breached any obligation under this Agreement if the Confidential In formation is disclosed to a third party when the Confidential Information: (d) subject to the provisions of Section 4, is used or disclosed pursuant to statutory duty, such as a public records act request, or an order, subpoena, di scovery request, or other lawful process issued by a court or other governmental authority of competen t jurisdiction or in a judicial proceeding
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding Sections 1 and 2, a Participating Entity shall not have breached any obligation under this Agreement if the Confidential Information is disclosed to a third party when the Confidential Information: (d) is used or disclosed pursuant to statutory duty, such as a public records act request, or an order, subpoena, discovery request, or other lawful process issued by any court or other governmental authority regardless of jurisdiction or in any judicial proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'of competent jurisdiction' when referring to a court or governmental authority, the modified text broadens the scope of permissible disclosures. It implies that a Participating Entity can disclose Confidential Information in response to an order from any court or governmental authority, even if that authority lacks proper jurisdiction. This could lead to disclosures that violate jurisdictional boundaries and undermine the protection of Confidential Information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45(a)(2) deals with subpoenas and dictates that a subpoena must be issued from a court with proper jurisdiction over the matter. By removing the requirement of 'competent jurisdiction,' the altered contract language implies compliance with subpoenas issued by courts lacking proper jurisdiction, potentially forcing disclosure of confidential information where it would not be legally required or appropriate, contradicting the spirit and intention of procedural rules governing jurisdiction and lawful process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts legal principles by removing the 'competent jurisdiction' requirement, potentially allowing compliance with subpoenas from courts lacking proper authority. This directly opposes the intent of procedural rules governing jurisdiction, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45, implicitly supports this by outlining the requirements for a subpoena, including its issuance from the correct court. The original text limited disclosure to courts with competent jurisdiction, aligning with the legal principle that a court must have jurisdiction to issue a valid order or subpoena. By removing this limitation, the changed text creates a legal contradiction and increases the risk of improper disclosure of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without giving effect to any choice or conflicts of law provision or rule that would cause the application of laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, explicitly including its choice of law provisions, even if those provisions would cause the application of the laws of another jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly including the choice of law provisions of New York, even if they lead to the application of another jurisdiction's laws, the contract creates uncertainty and potential conflicts. The original text aims to avoid conflicts of law, whereas the modified text embraces them, which could lead to disputes over which jurisdiction's laws apply.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law to govern their contracts under certain conditions. However, the modified text's explicit inclusion of choice of law provisions, even if they lead to the application of another jurisdiction's laws, could contradict the intent and limitations of this statute. While parties can generally choose New York law, this statute doesn't necessarily override mandatory rules of other jurisdictions that might apply based on specific circumstances (e.g., where the contract is performed). The original avoids conflict, the modification seeks it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Consolidated Laws, General Obligations Law - GOB § 5-1401 | FindLaw New York Consolidated Laws, General Obligations Law - GOB § 5-1401. Choice of law Current as of January 01, 2024 | Updated by FindLaw Staff 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1-301 of the uniform commercial code , may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1-301 of the uniform commercial code . 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking. perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by explicitly including the choice of law provisions, even if they lead to the application of another jurisdiction's laws. This undermines the original intent to avoid conflicts of law. New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 2, allows parties to choose New York law to govern their contracts. However, the changed text introduces ambiguity and potential conflict by suggesting that another jurisdiction's laws might apply, contradicting the purpose of choosing New York law in the first place and making enforcement unpredictable.
